coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and sensitive “on-off-on” probes for iron(III) ion and apoferritin detection and imaging in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Han, Cuiping
Wang, Ru
Wang, Keying
Xu, Huiting
Sui, Meirong
Li, Jingjing
Xu, Kai
coredata.dc:description: Highly blue luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 42.3% were prepared by an efficient one-step pyrolytic route from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and urea. The as-synthesized N-CDs were demonstrated as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free, selective and sensitive recognition of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0.5μM to 2mM and a detection limit of 13.6nM due to Fe3+-quenched fluorescence (turn-off). The quenched fluorescence could be turned on after the addition of apoferritin owing to the removal of ferric species from the surface of N-CDs by apoferritin, making complex N-CDs/Fe3+ a selective apoferritin probe with a linear range of 0.1–25μM and a detection limit as low as 2.6nM. In addition, the application of this novel N-CDs-based probe for imaging Fe3+ ions and apoferritin in living cells suggest that this sensing system has great potential applications in biosensing, bioimaging, and many other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Iron(III)
Apoferritin
On-off-on
Sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Research Foundation of Jiangsu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81301254 and 81470075 ), Natural Science Research Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20130217 ), and the Science and Technology Development Program of Xuzhou (No. KC14SM088 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic illustration of the synthesis of N-CDs and the strategy for Fe3+ and apoferritin detection.
(A) TEM image and size distribution (inset) of as-prepared N-CDs. (B) The XRD pattern, (C) FT-IR spectrum and (D) XPS spectrum of N-CDs sample.

(A) Fluorescence image of N-CDs, and N-CDs with various metal ions, which were taken under the illumination of a UV light (365nm). (B) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs upon addition of metal salts (5mM). (C) The various fluorescence intensity ratio (I0−I)/I0 of the N-CDs solution in the presence of various individual metal ions (from 1 to 12: Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Hg2+. (D) Representative fluorescence emission spectra of N-CDs in the presence of increasing Fe3+concentrations (0–5mM). (E) The relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ from 0 to 5mM. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements. Inset is Stern-Volmer plot of the quenching of the fluorescence of N-CDs by Fe3+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/iron upon addition of proteins (25μM) with excitation at 380nm. (B) Fluorescence change ratio (I−I0)/I0 of N-CDs and Fe3+-chelated N-CDs in the presence of 25μM relevant proteins. (C) Fluorescence response of the Fe3+-chelated N-CDs to apoferritin at varied concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25μM). (D) The plot of the fluorescence intensity against the apoferritin concentration in the range of 0.1–25μM. The linear equation was found to be C/I=0.00245C+0.000108 with a correlation coefficient of 0.993, where I refers to the fluorescence intensity and C refers to the concentration of apoferritin.

Fluorescence images of HO-8910 cells: (A) fluorescence image of cells after incubation with N-CDs (0.5mg/mL) at 37°C for 1h; (B) fluorescence image of N-CDs treated cells after incubation with Fe(NO3)3 (0.5mM) at 37°C for 1h; (C) fluorescence image after further incubation with apoferritin (25μM) for 1h. The fluorescence images were acquired with blue light excitation.

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent carbon dots as selective and sensitive “on-off-on” probes for iron(III) ion and apoferritin detection and imaging in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Radiology, Affiliated Hospital of Xuzhou Medical University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou 221004, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Imaging, Xuzhou Medical University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly blue luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with a fluorescence quantum yield of 42.3% were prepared by an efficient one-step pyrolytic route from ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid and urea. The as-synthesized N-CDs were demonstrated as an effective fluorescent probe for label-free, selective and sensitive recognition of Fe3+ with a linear range of 0.5μM to 2mM and a detection limit of 13.6nM due to Fe3+-quenched fluorescence (turn-off). The quenched fluorescence could be turned on after the addition of apoferritin owing to the removal of ferric species from the surface of N-CDs by apoferritin, making complex N-CDs/Fe3+ a selective apoferritin probe with a linear range of 0.1–25μM and a detection limit as low as 2.6nM. In addition, the application of this novel N-CDs-based probe for imaging Fe3+ ions and apoferritin in living cells suggest that this sensing system has great potential applications in biosensing, bioimaging, and many other fields.

ce:section.ce:para: During the recent years, a novel type of luminescent carbon nanomaterial, zero-dimensional carbon dots (CDs) has gained considerable attention of scientific community because of their unique properties, such as robust chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, etc. (Esteves da Silva and Goncalves, 2011; Qu et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2015). All of these properties make CDs to be excellent candidates for the development of novel and sensitive sensors in current research (Luo et al., 2014; Zhu et al., 2013; Gao et al., 2015). Fluorescent CDs with ion-responsive properties that can selectively respond to specific ions are of extreme interest and importance amongst the smart sensors because of the crucial roles of ions in life processes and health. In this context, Hg2+ (Zhou et al., 2012; Zhang and Chen, 2014; Gonçalves et al., 2012; Yuan et al., 2014), Cu2+ (Zhao et al., 2014; Liu et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2013; Salinas-Castillo et al., 2013), and Pb2+-responsive (Wee et al., 2013) CDs have been constructed. Fe3+ ion is an important metal ion in life and plays significant roles in oxygen uptake, oxygen metabolism, and electronic transfer (Lynch, 1997). But excessive iron accumulate in body can lead to tissue damage, organ failure and eventually death (Fix and Kowdley, 2008). Ferritin is an iron-binding glycoprotein which plays a key role in the storage and transport of iron in biological systems. The protein component, apoferritin, combined with iron are involved in many important biological processes and play important roles in maintaining the balance of Fe3+ ions in biological systems (Kidane et al., 2006). Both Fe3+ and apoferritin are indispensable in biology. Their deficiency or excess from the normal permissible limit can induce serious disorders. Therefore, the development of effective sensing systems for qualitative and quantitative determination of Fe3+ and apoferritin is of great significance for clinical, medical and environmental concerns. Many efforts have been devoted to this topic and have produced a few carbon-based luminescence techniques for Fe detection (Wang et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013a, b; Xu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014; Chandra et al., 2016; Chen et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2015). Although these fluorescent carbon-based sensors hold great promise in biological and chemical sensing applications, most of these CDs are suffer from the complicated synthesis operations, long reaction time, high reaction temperature or producing CDs with low quantum yield. So much work is still needed to improve the fluorescence quantum yield and explore the full potential of these nanomaterials for developing advanced smart sensors. To our knowledge, the direct use of label-free CDs-based fluorescent sensor as a Fe-responsive system and, subsequently, as a secondary sensor for apoferritin has not been previously reported.

In this work, we report a novel nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) fluorescent sensor for label-free detection of Fe3+ ion and apoferritin with high sensitivity and selectivity. Earlier studies revealed that the composition and surface function groups had a profound effect on the luminescence efficiency and sensing property of CDs (Dhenadhayalan and Lin, 2015). On this basis, it can be reasonably expected that by properly choosing starting material, highly photoluminescent CDs and specific sensing of analytes can be achieved. Herein, we present a facile and large-scale strategy for the synthesis of N-CDs with a quantum yield of 42.3% by pyrolyzing low-cost ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and urea at 150°C in air. The obtained N-CDs exhibited strong blue emission, high stability, and low cytotoxicity. The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs was effectively quenched by ferric species (turn-off) and restored reversibly by the addition of apoferritin (turn-on). Based on this phenomenon, an “on-off-on” approach for detection of Fe3+and apoferritin with high sensitivity and selectivity using N-CDs as fluorescent probes was performed ( Fig. 1). The application of this novel N-CDs-based probe for imaging Fe3+ and apoferritin in living cells was also described.

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), urea, ethylene glycol and metal nitrate salts were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Beijing, China). Aqueous solutions were all prepared using ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) prepared from a Millipore water purification system (Millipore Corp., Bedford, USA). All chemicals and solvents were obtained from the commercial sources and used directly without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopes (TEM) were obtained on FEI Tecnai F20. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker Optics, Germany) using KBr as a sample matrix. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a D8 Advance spectroscopy (Bruker AXS Corporation, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was measured by a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) with monochromatized Al Kα radiation using C 1s (284.8eV) as the reference. The UV–vis spectroscopy measurements were performed on a T6 series UV–vis spectrophotometer (Puxi Co., Beijing, China). The fluorescence spectra were detected using a LS-55 Fluorescence Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Fremont, CA, USA) and the fluorescence quantum yield was determined with quinoline sulfate as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, EDTA (0.2mM) and urea (2mM) were added into ethylene glycol (10mL). The reaction was heated from room temperature to 150°C in an oil bath and held at 150°C for 3h with vigorous stirring. The solution changed colorless to yellow and finally to brown. Then the reaction flask was taken out of the oil bath and naturally cooled to room temperature. The product, which was brown and transparent, was placed in a dialysis bag (cutoff Mn: 1.0kDa) and dialyzed against water for 2 days to remove small molecules. And finally, N-CDs were dried in vacuo to give a dark brown solid.

The N-CD powder was dissolved in 10mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH=7.2) with a concentration of 0.1mg/mL. Metal ions aqueous solutions containing Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+ and Hg2+ were prepared, respectively, with a concentration of 5mM. In a typical assay, 10μL of metal ion solution was mixed with 90μL of the N-CD solution, and equilibrated for 5min at room temperature before the spectral measurements. The sensitivity for Fe3+ was confirmed by adding of serial concentrations of Fe3+ in a similar way. The control sample was prepared by mixing 10μL of pure water with 90μL of the N-CD solution.

For the apoferritin detection, 90μL of the N-CD solution (0.1mg/mL) was mixed with 10μL Fe3+ solution (1mM) for 5min, and then 10μL of serial concentration of apoferritin were added. After equilibrated for 5min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The selectivity for apoferritin was confirmed by adding other proteins stock solutions instead of apoferritin in a similar way. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 380nm and all experiments were performed at room temperature.

HO-8910 cells were incubated in RPMI1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air, which were seeded in a flat-bottom 6-well plate (1×104 cells/well) with glass coverslips in 2mL culture medium. After overnight incubation, the cells were treated with N-CDs for 1h at 37°C and then washed with PBS buffer (pH=7.4) to remove excess N-CDs. The treated cells were then incubated with Fe3+ for another hour. Then the cells on the plate were washed with PBS buffer and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde in PBS buffer for 15min at room temperature. After being washed three times with PBS buffer, the cells were imaged under a fluorescence microscope. The cells loaded with N-CDs/Fe3+ were further treated with apoferritin (25μM) for 1h for apoferritin imaging. Competition study with native transferrin was performed by adding 50μM of the protein to the cell culture medium 1h before apoferritin addition. Cells incubated with pure N-CDs for 1h served as a control.

The N-CDs were prepared by a facile and general strategy using one-step direct solvothermal reaction by carbonization of EDTA and urea in ethylene glycol at 150°C for 3h (Fig. 1). The precursor of EDTA combined with urea are economic and rich in multifunctional groups, providing both carbon and nitrogen sources for the formation of fluorescent N-CDs with high quantum yield. Compared with the treatment EDTA-2Na at 400°C in a N2 atmosphere (Pan et al., 2010), our route is simple and makes it possible to prepare N-CDs on a large scale.

Control experiments show that EDTA and urea were very important for the formation of highly fluorescent N-CDs (Table S1, Figs. S1 and S2). First, if the reactants only contained EDTA, the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of prepared N-CDs were less than 5%. However, if urea was present, the QYs of most prepared CDs can be over 10%. In the reaction process, urea used both as the precursor for N-dopant and the passivation agent, which both greatly contribute to the fluorescent enhancement of N-CDs (Liu et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013c). Similar to that of doped carbon dots (Ding et al., 2014), the fluorescent performance of N-CDs strongly depend on their component and surface states. The changes in the molar ratio of two reactants by using different amount of urea result in the alteration of the content of carbon and nitrogen-rich groups, which would affect the surface state of N-CDs. When increasing the amount of urea (molar ratio of EDTA to urea changes from 1:0 to 1:10), a significant fluorescent enhancement with a blue-shift of N-CDs was observed, which may result from the increasing amount of nitrogen content in N-CDs, similar to the previous reported N-doped graphene quantum dots whose electron affinity of N atoms make contribution to the blue-shift emission (Li et al., 2012). As measured from the fluorescent spectra (Fig. S2), the optimal molar ratio of EDTA and urea was 1:10.

Reaction time is another important parameter for N-CDs synthesis. Figs. S3 and S4 depict the morphology and the fluorescence properties of N-CDs synthesized at various different times. In a modest reaction time, polymer-like particles are formed and the fluorescence arises from the surface/molecule state (perhaps owing to amide-containing fluorophores) (Zhu et al., 2013). As the reaction progressed, carbogenic N-CDs are formed owing to further carbonization, and the fluorescence is derived from the synergistic effect of the carbogenic core and the surface/molecule state. The optimal reaction time is found to be 3h as it produces the highest fluorescence intensity.

Based on the above results and previous report (Zhou et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013c), we propose the growth mechanism of N-CDs. The dehydration–condensation reaction may first occur while heating the EDTA and urea at 150°C, whereupon it may polymerize to form polymer-like nanoparticles. Afterwards, the polymers are carbonized to form carbon nuclei, and the carbon nuclei subsequently form nanocrystalline N-doped CDs with the extending reaction time.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image clearly indicates that the formed N-CDs were spherical in shape with the average size of 2.8nm ( Fig. 2A). XRD pattern (Fig. 2B) shows a broad diffraction peak at about 23° (2θ), which corresponds to amorphous carbon ordered in a considerably random fashion the disorder carbon of N-CD (Wei et al., 2014). Surface status information on N-CDs was obtained from FI-TR spectra (Fig. 2C). In the FT-IR analysis of N-CDs, the following were observed: stretching vibrations of C–N at 1422cm−1, asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C at 1125cm−1, bending vibrations of N-H at 1575cm−1, and the vibrational absorption band of C=O at 1678cm−1. Broad absorption bands at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned to stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H. Those absorption bands indicate that there exist lots of amino-containing functional groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, which results in the N-CDs having good hydrophilic properties.

To further confirm the structure information on N-CDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed. The XPS full scan spectrum of N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 2D, presents three predominant peaks at 531, 399, and 284eV, which corresponds to the binding energies of O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively. This result indicates that the produced N-CDs are mainly composed of three elements of C, O and N. The contents of the three elements in the sample were 66.38% (C), 19.89% (O) and 13.73% (N), respectively (Table S1). The N/C atomic ratio was calculated to be 20.68%, indicating the high doping concentration of nitrogen in N-CDs. The high-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s spectra further confirm that C–C/C=C, C–N–C, C–O and C=O/C=N, C=O, C–OH/C–O–C, N-C3 and N–H bonds exhibit in N-CDs (Fig. S5). The characterizations from FTIR and XPS indicated that the surface of the as-synthesized N-CDs is functionalized by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups, which could interact with metal ions.

To explore the optical properties, UV–vis absorption and emission spectra of N-CDs were recorded. The N-CDs aqueous solution has two obvious UV–vis absorbance bands at 280 and 320nm, which can be assigned to the π–π* transition corresponding to carbon core and the n–π* transition corresponding to the carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface, respectively (Fig. S6) (Wang and Hu, 2014). The N-CDs solution was yellowish, transparent, and clear under sunlight, and exhibited bright blue luminescence under UV excitation (365nm) that can be easily seen with the naked eye (inset in Fig. S7B), indicating that the N-CDs had an excellent fluorescence property. The emission spectra of N-CDs exhibit a typical excitation-dependent feature as depicted in Fig. S7A; the emission peak gradually shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of the excitation wavelength, with an excitation maximum at 380nm and emission maximum at 443nm (Fig. S7B). The excitation-dependent fluorescent behavior has also been reported in previous studies of other fluorescent carbon-based nanoparticles, which are likely due to the different emissive sites on N-CDs (Tang et al., 2012; Zhu et al., 2011). The fluorescence quantum yield measured with quinoline sulfate as the reference was 42.3%, which was higher than that of some other N-doped CDs (less than 20%) (Gong et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Dong et al., 2014).

The fluorescence of N-CDs exhibited remarkable stability. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength almost did not change when the pH values were in the range of 1–12 (Fig. S8). Such a wide pH stability range makes the N-CDs relevant for numerous biological applications. After 2.5h of continuous excitation at 380nm (light source: 450W Xe lamp, excitation slit: 7.5nm), the fluorescence bands showed fewer fluctuations and the intensity was nearly constant under the same test conditions (Fig. S9), suggesting their outstanding photostability. The as-prepared N-CDs solution can keep stable for more than several months in air at room temperature (Fig. S10). The good photostability of N-CDs guarantees the stability of the fluorescence signal, ensuring the accuracy of the detection results.

To evaluate the sensing ability of N-CDs toward metal ions, changes in the fluorescence properties of N-CDs upon the addition of various metal nitrate salts (5mM) were measured ( Fig. 3). The changes in fluorescence intensity of N-CDs upon addition of a particular metal ions are shown in Fig. 3B, and the fluorescence change ratio (I0−I)/I0 is displayed in Fig. 3C. Here I0 and I are the fluorescent intensities of N-CDs at 443nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The obtained N-CDs showed high selectivity toward Fe3+. The presence of Fe3+ remarkably quenched the emission intensity with a fluorescence change ratio of 97%. Under the same conditions, the addition of other metal ions, including Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Mg2+, Mn2+ and Hg2+ (5mM), resulted in small or no obvious fluorescence changes (defined as the relative fluorescence change ratio of 1–13% as compared to blank). These results clearly demonstrated that the N-CDs-based fluorescent sensor was highly selective toward Fe3+ over the other metal ions. This Fe3+ recognition can be easily observed by the naked eye. As Fig. 3A shows, the N-CDs solution emitted strong blue color with no metal ion added. In the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence was quenched completely, while no notable fluorescence quenching was observed in the presence of other tested ions.

Possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ was analyzed from the point of view of their ion selective chemical structure and charge/energy transfer effect. According to the XPS results, oxygen is main component of the N-CDs in comparison to nitrogen, and the C–OH/C–O–C groups are about 87% of oxygen-containing groups. The FTIR data also demonstrated that there were plentiful hydroxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs. The hydroxyl groups on carbon dots could react with Fe3+ and form complexes due to coordination (Zhang et al., 2013; Chen et al., 2016). The complexes may change the electronic structure of N-CDs and affect the distribution of excitons, which accelerate the nonradiative recombination of the excitons through effective photoelectron or energy transfer, further results in the fluorescence quenching (Wang et al., 2012; Qu et al., 2013a, b; Xu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014).

Control experiment using CDs-MA (prepared using l-malic acid as carbon precursor with the same procedure as that of N-CDs, see Supplementary material) as fluorescence probe shows that the presence of Fe3+ has little quenching effect on the fluorescent property of CDs-MA in the same measuring condition. Instead, Ag+ and Hg2+ ions show significant effect on fluorescence quenching among the metal ions studied (Fig. S11). These results clearly illustrate that the sensing property of CDs strongly depends on the carbon precursor and the functional groups present on the CDs.

To investigate the potential practical application of the proposed sensing nanoprobe, the selective response to 5mM Fe3+ in complex matrices that might interfere with Fe3+ detection in certain biological samples, including proteins (BSA, 0.1mg/mL), amino acids (phenylalanine and leucine, 1mM), and biologically relevant ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl−,
HPO 3
2 −
, and
HPO 3 −
ions (10mM) were evaluated. As Fig. S12 illustrates, the label-free N-CDs-based fluorescent sensor performed well when challenged in complex matrices. The matrices sample had only a little effect on the analysis, as indicated by a slight variation in the fluorescence intensity (approximately 5%), which probably results from physical adsorption or other nonspecific interference. The results show that N-CDs has good antidisturbance ability and a remarkable specificity towards Fe3+.

In addition to the high selectivity of N-CDs towards Fe3+, their sensitivity was investigated. Upon addition of Fe3+ at a 0.5μM to 5mM concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs solution was gradually quenched (Fig. 3D). Fig. 3E presented the relationship of the changes of fluorescence intensity with the concentration of Fe3+. At low concentrations the quenching of fluorescence in solution by Fe3+ follows a typical Stern-Volmer equation: I 0/I=1+Ksv [Fe3+], where I 0 is the fluorescence intensity without Fe3+, I is the fluorescence intensity observed in the presence of Fe3+ and Ksv is the static Stern-Volmer constant (Lakowicz, 1999). The analysis of the Stern-Volmer plot show that they follow a linear trend with a Ksv =1.86×104 M−1 (Fig. 3E inset). Therefore, judging from the relatively large magnitude of the Stern–Volmer constant, the quenching provoked by Fe3+ is probably due to static quenching arising from the formation of a stable non-fluorescent complex between CD and Fe3+ (Goncalves et al., 2010). The detection limit (DL) of 13.6nM was calculated according to the IUPAC 3σ criterion (DL=3SD/S, where SD is the standard error of the intercept and S is the slope of the calibration curve).
Compared with the results using different carbon-based fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+ in the recent years, our method showed better linear ranges and detection limits (Table S3).

The iron storage protein ferritin is composed of 24 subunits of two types, H and L, which assemble to form a hollow protein shell capable of encapsulating up to 4500 Fe atoms in the form of a hydrous ferric oxide mineral core (Proulx-Curry and Chasteen, 1995). Therefore, the N-CDs/Fe3+ system can be explored to probe apoferritin based on the competition mechanism between apoferritin, iron species, and N-CDs.

The fluorescent characteristics of the Fe3+-chelated N-CDs upon addition of various proteins, such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), ferritin, apoferritin lactoferrin and apolactoferrin (25μM) in the 10mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.2) were investigated ( Fig. 4). As expected, upon addition of apoferritin to the solution of N-CDs/Fe3+, the quenched fluorescence of N-CDs was recovered immediately with a fluorescence intensity change ratio (I0−I)/I0 of 1.33±0.092, whereas only slight changes were observed after treatment with the BSA and other iron-rich proteins (ferritin and lactoferrin) (Fig. 4A and B). The fluorescence enhancement in the presence of apoferritin is might be due to the stronger binding between apoferritin and iron species, which disturbs the interaction between iron and N-CDs, and then fluorescence of N-CDs restored. For further demonstrate above explanation, the zeta-potential measurements were carried out and the results were showed in Table S4. Along with the addition of Fe3+, the negative charge density of N-CDs declined (from −15.83 to −1.64). This result suggests that Fe3+ conjugated to the surface of N-CDs. However, the addition of apoferritin increased the negative zeta-potential from −1.64 to −9.79, which indicated that iron separated from the surface of N-CDs and CDs re-exposed. Additionally, control experiments without Fe3+ complexation under the same experimental conditions revealed that the fluorescence intensity of pure N-CDs had a negligible change in the presence of apoferritin (Fig. 4B). This clearly suggested that the fluorescence enhancement of Fe3+-chelated N-CDs solution was actually attributable to the high binding affinity of apoferritin towards ferric species, which removed Fe3+ from the surface of N-CDs. It was found that apolactoferrin could also recover the fluorescence of the Fe3+-chelated N-CDs, however, the fluorescence intensity change ratio (0.42±0.029) is relatively low compared with that of apoferritin at the same concentration. This behavior can be explained by iron-binding capacity of proteins. Naturally, lactoferrin molecule possesses two specific iron-binding sites localized in each of the two homologous globular domains named N-and C-lobes. Each lactoferrin molecule can reversibly bind with two ions of iron (Moore et al., 1997). For ferritin, up to 4500 iron atoms can be stored within the protein shell (Proulx-Curry and Chasteen, 1995). Because of higher affinity for iron, apoferritin binds more iron than apolactoferrin at same concentration, which finally results in apoferritin selectively recover the luminescence of the Fe3+-chelated N-CDs.

Fig. 4C presents the fluorescence spectra versus the concentration of apoferritin. The fluorescence intensity increased with increasing concentrations of apoferritin. A good linear correlation is shown under the concentration range from 0.1 to 25μM ( Fig. 5D). The limit of detection was estimated to be 2.6nM based on the IUPAC 3σ criterion. These observations indicate that Fe3+-chelated N-CDs can be used as fluorescent nanosensors for ultrasensitive and selective detection of apoferritin. This fluorescence turn-on nanosensor can eliminate the disturbance from the detection medium and provides an idea design strategy for detecting other biological molecules with N-CDs.

Before biological application, the cytotoxicity of N-CDs toward HO-8910 cell (a human ovarian cancer cell line) was performed by the conventional MTS assay (Fig. S13). The result shows that after 48h of incubation with 3mg/mL N-CDs, the cell viability is still over 85%. The bright-field optical images also validated no morphological difference between N-CDs-treated groups and the control groups (Fig. S14). Because the maximum concentration tested was larger than the necessary quantity and the exposure times were much longer for the bioimaging study, thus N-CDs possess low cytotoxicity and could act as an excellent bioimaging reagent.

Finally, we have evaluated the ability of the sensor to operate in live cells. Fig. 5 shows fluorescence images of intracellular imaging in live HO-8910 (a human ovarian cancer cell line) cells. It was found that the cells incubated with only N-CDs exhibited strong blue fluorescence (Fig. 5A), whereas those incubated with N-CDs and further with Fe3+ showed weak intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 5B), demonstrating that the N-CDs could penetrate the cells membrane and translocate into cells by endocytosis and bind to intracellular Fe3+. When the cells loaded with N-CDs/Fe3+ were further treated with apoferritin, intracellular fluorescence enhancement was observed (Fig. 5C). Competition studies with transferrin showed that the cellular uptake of apoferritin involves transferrin receptor-mediated endocytosis (Fig. S15). Although the detailed cell uptake mechanism was still unclear, the possible explanation for the fluorescence enhancement process is: endocytosed apoferritin has strong binding preference toward Fe3+ and compete with N-CDs to form ferritin complex, which disturbs the interaction between Fe3+ and N-CDs and lead to an “off-on” fluorescence switching. These results suggest that N-CDs successfully perform selective Fe3+ and apoferritin imaging in living cells despite the presence of many potentially interfering substances, such as proteins and amino acids.

In conclusion, a facile method has been established to fabricate monodisperse N-CDs by carbonization of EDTA and urea within 3h. The preparation process is convenient, rapid, and the starting material is green, low cost and eco-friendly. The obtained N-CDs have been evaluated as a label-free fluorescent nanoprobe for detection of Fe3+and apoferritin in aqueous medium by a simple on-off-on fluorescent method with remarkable specificity and sensitivity. Importantly, fluorescence imaging experiments in living cells demonstrated the value of this probe for practical applications in biological systems. Given its simple synthesis, excellent biocompatibility and superior optical properties, the proposed CDs-based sensing strategy could promote a potentially convenient assay for disease-related molecular targets and could find practical applications in biotechnology and life science.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81301254 and 81470075), Natural Science Research Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20130217), and the Science and Technology Development Program of Xuzhou (No. KC14SM088).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at 10.1016/j.bios.2016.04.066.
